% A contamination-robust infrared-excess technosignature search around white
% dwarfs: methodology and projected sensitivity.
%
% Drafted in the `article` class so it compiles anywhere; the submission version
% targets MNRAS or PASP (swap to the journal class). All quantitative results are
% auto-generated from the pipeline into numbers.tex (`make paper-numbers`), so no
% figure is hand-transcribed.
\documentclass[11pt]{article}
\usepackage[margin=1in]{geometry}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,graphicx,natbib,booktabs,hyperref,siunitx,xspace,xcolor}
\newcommand{\TODO}[1]{\textcolor{red}{[#1]}}
\newcommand{\wone}{$W1$\xspace}
\newcommand{\wtwo}{$W2$\xspace}
\bibliographystyle{plainnat}
\input{numbers}

\title{A contamination-robust search for infrared-excess technosignatures
around white dwarfs: an empirical search of the \SciDist\,pc sample and projected
sensitivity}
\author{[Author list]}
\date{\today}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
Waste heat from a planetary-scale energy-collecting structure (a Dyson sphere or
swarm) is a classic technosignature that would appear as anomalous infrared
excess. Systematic photometric searches have so far targeted main-sequence
stars, most prominently Project Hephaistos, whose candidates proved vulnerable
to background contamination. White dwarfs have been considered only
theoretically, despite being uniquely favourable targets: their photospheres are
near-blackbody and fully specified by Gaia astrometry and effective temperature,
so an infrared excess is sharply defined, and the sole significant natural source
of white-dwarf infrared excess --- a dusty debris disk --- is already catalogued
and usable as a labelled control. We present the design, an open implementation,
and the projected sensitivity of the first systematic catalogue search for
infrared-excess technosignatures around white dwarfs, combining the Gaia EDR3
white-dwarf catalogue ($\sim\NhighConf$ high-confidence white dwarfs) with
CatWISE2020, unWISE, AllWISE and 2MASS photometry. Our central methodological
contribution is a contamination-robust vetting funnel whose decisive, novel cut
is a Gaia--WISE \emph{co-movement} test: it requires the infrared source to share
the white dwarf's large proper motion, rejecting the chance-aligned background
interlopers that defeated earlier broadband searches. At the CatWISE2020 source
density a $\sim$\SI{3}{arcsec} match implies a $\sim\ChanceRate\%$ chance-alignment
rate per white dwarf; the co-movement cut removes $\RemovedFrac\%$ of these (a
factor of $\RejectionFactor$), with near-complete rejection for fast-moving white
dwarfs. Using a realistic
population model calibrated to the published Gaia and WISE statistics and
end-to-end injection--recovery, we forecast that a null search of the
$\NhundredPc$ white dwarfs within \SI{100}{pc} --- of which $\sim\WiseFrac\%$
($\NeffWise$) are detected by CatWISE2020 --- would constrain the occurrence of
warm ($T_{\rm dust}\sim\SI{500}{}$--$\SI{1000}{K}$), substantial
($\tau\gtrsim0.1$) Dyson-swarm-like excess to $f<\fUpperBest$ at 95\% confidence,
the first such limit for the white-dwarf population. Applying the pipeline to the
real \SciDist\,pc sample, we search $\SciNsearchable$ white dwarfs with CatWISE2020
and 2MASS or Gaia photometry, recover $\SciNexcess$ with infrared excess (the
warm-dust debris-disk population), and place an empirical 95\% occurrence-rate
limit of $f<\SciFupper$, with the warm debris-disk population separated by
the dust locus and $\SciKcand$
cool-excess candidates remaining for follow-up. The empirical limit is nearly two
orders of magnitude weaker than the forecast, set mainly by the residual
cool-excess candidate population that two-band photometry cannot reject. We
emphasise that broadband $W1$/$W2$ photometry cannot uniquely
distinguish a Dyson swarm from warm dust or an unresolved brown-dwarf companion
--- the latter the dominant astrophysical false positive at the candidate level;
surviving sources are prioritised follow-up targets, not detections. To move
beyond the inherent degeneracy of a single infrared-excess axis, we further
develop a multi-modal anomaly framework that scores each white dwarf on several
independent and physically distinct axes --- an ultraviolet deficit, an
energy-balance match between ultraviolet absorption and infrared re-emission, and
optical and mid-infrared variability --- and promotes only objects flagged on two
or more axes at once. The energy-balance axis, which tests the defining
thermodynamic signature of a reprocessing structure, is the most discriminating in
principle, and the framework converts a confounder-dominated one-dimensional
candidate list into a sharply ranked one.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}

A sufficiently energy-intensive technological civilisation may surround its host
star, in whole or part, with energy-collecting structures
\citep[a Dyson sphere or swarm;][]{Dyson1960}. Thermodynamics requires that the
intercepted starlight be re-radiated as waste heat, so such a structure betrays
itself through anomalous mid-infrared emission superimposed on (or replacing) the
stellar spectral energy distribution \citep[SED;][]{Wright2014}. This makes
infrared excess one of the few technosignatures already accessible in existing
all-sky survey data.

The most ambitious recent search, Project Hephaistos
\citep[][]{Suazo2024}, cross-matched Gaia DR3, 2MASS and AllWISE for five million
main-sequence stars and identified seven partial-Dyson-sphere candidates. Those
candidates were, however, subsequently shown to be consistent with background
contamination --- blended or chance-aligned infrared sources --- rather than
genuine circumstellar excess \citep{Suazo2024contam}. The episode underscores
that for broadband infrared searches the dominant challenge is not detecting
excess but proving that an apparent excess is (i) real and (ii) physically
associated with the target star.

White dwarfs sidestep much of this difficulty, yet they have never been the
subject of a systematic infrared-excess technosignature search. Two properties
make them exceptionally clean targets. First, a white-dwarf photosphere is
essentially a blackbody whose absolute SED is fixed by the effective temperature
and radius; with a Gaia parallax and a photometric or spectroscopic $T_{\rm eff}$,
the expected \wone/\wtwo flux is predicted to $\sim$5 per cent (our adopted
systematic floor), so an excess is sharply defined. Second, the only astrophysically common source of white-dwarf
infrared excess is a dusty debris disk produced by tidally disrupted planetesimals
\citep[e.g.][]{Debes2011,Dennihy2020,Xu2021,MadurgaFavieres2024,MurilloOjeda2026},
and those disks are already catalogued. Rather than a nuisance, this single,
well-characterised confounder is an asset: it provides a labelled control
population that can be \emph{subtracted}, leaving any residual anomalies as
technosignature candidates. White dwarfs are also of independent interest as
hosts: a post-main-sequence civilisation might track its cooling star inward, and
the compact, low-luminosity white dwarf admits cooler, more compact structures
\citep{Zackrisson2022,DysonHR2026}.

In this paper we (i) set out the design of a contamination-robust white-dwarf
infrared-excess search, (ii) introduce and validate a Gaia--WISE co-movement cut
that directly targets the Hephaistos failure mode, (iii) release a fully
reproducible open-source pipeline, (iv) forecast the occurrence-rate sensitivity,
(v) carry out the search empirically on the \SciDist\,pc white-dwarf sample, and
(vi) extend it into a multi-modal anomaly framework that combines the infrared
excess with independent ultraviolet-deficit, energy-balance and variability axes
so that the most interesting candidates are those anomalous on several physically
distinct grounds at once.
Section~\ref{sec:data} describes the input catalogues; Section~\ref{sec:methods}
the SED prediction, excess statistic, contamination funnel and
dust/technosignature discriminator; Section~\ref{sec:validation} the
injection--recovery validation; Section~\ref{sec:forecast} the projected
sensitivity; Section~\ref{sec:empirical} the empirical search and its results;
and Section~\ref{sec:discussion} the interpretation, limitations and outlook.

\section{Data}
\label{sec:data}

\paragraph{Parent sample.} We adopt the Gaia EDR3 white-dwarf catalogue of
\citet{GentileFusillo2021}, selecting the $\sim\NhighConf$ high-confidence white
dwarfs ($P_{\rm WD}>0.75$, tightened to $0.9$ for the search), each with a
photometric $T_{\rm eff}$, $\log g$ and atmosphere type. The
$\sim\NhundredPc$ white dwarfs within \SI{100}{pc} form a nearly complete,
well-characterised subsample that anchors our sensitivity forecast.

\paragraph{Infrared photometry.} The primary infrared photometry is CatWISE2020
\citep[][\wone, \wtwo, with proper motions]{Marocco2021}, which is deeper than
AllWISE ($5\sigma$ limits $W1=\WoneDepth$, $W2=\WtwoDepth$, Vega) and provides
the proper motions our co-movement test requires. We use unWISE
\citep{Schlafly2019} forced photometry as an independent reduction to confirm
that excesses survive deblending, AllWISE \citep{Cutri2013} for its quality flags
and the neighbourhood multiplicity used in crowding rejection, and 2MASS for the
near-infrared anchor. All access is catalogue-scale through Virtual Observatory
TAP services and the CDS X-Match service.

\paragraph{Control debris-disk catalogues.} We assemble published white-dwarf
debris-disk / infrared-excess catalogues
\citep{Debes2011,Dennihy2020,Xu2021,MadurgaFavieres2024,MurilloOjeda2026} into a
single labelled control sample, used both to validate completeness (the pipeline
must recover known excesses) and to subtract the natural population before
reporting candidates.

\section{Methods}
\label{sec:methods}

\subsection{Photospheric SED prediction}
For each white dwarf we predict the photospheric \wone and \wtwo flux from a
blackbody of temperature $T_{\rm eff}$, normalised by a single solid-angle factor.
The scale is fit preferentially to the 2MASS $J/H/K_s$ photometry, which lies
closest to the Rayleigh--Jeans tail; where 2MASS is unavailable we fall back to
the Gaia $BP/RP/G$ bands, which enlarges the searchable sample at the cost of a
larger systematic (the optical white-dwarf SED departs from a blackbody through
the Balmer features and, for hot white dwarfs, the proximity of the spectral
peak). We evaluate the full Planck function rather than the Rayleigh--Jeans
limit, since for cool white dwarfs ($T_{\rm eff}\lesssim5000$\,K) \wone/\wtwo lie
shortward of the pure Rayleigh--Jeans regime. A constant systematic floor
($\sigma_{\rm sys}=0.05$\,mag, i.e.\ $\sim5\%$ in flux) absorbs the model and
zero-point uncertainty; this sets the precision to which the photospheric
\wone/\wtwo is predicted. Interstellar reddening is modest over the \SciDist\,pc
search volume --- the Local Bubble keeps $E(B-V)\lesssim0.02$ within
\SI{100}{pc}, rising to $E(B-V)\sim0.05$ for the more distant and low-latitude
sightlines out to \SI{200}{pc} --- and for the bulk of the sample remains
dominated by $\sigma_{\rm sys}$; nonetheless it reddens the anchor in the same
sense as a genuine excess, so it is a recognised sub-dominant systematic that
grows with distance and that we flag as a limitation of the wider search. We
cross-check against Montreal/Bergeron model-atmosphere photometry as a robustness
test.

\subsection{Infrared-excess statistic}
\label{sec:excess}
The per-band excess significance is
\begin{equation}
  \chi_B = \frac{F_{\mathrm{obs},B} - F_{\mathrm{pred},B}}{\sigma_B},\qquad
  \sigma_B^2 = \sigma_{\mathrm{phot},B}^2 + \sigma_{\mathrm{sys}}^2,
\end{equation}
with a systematic floor $\sigma_{\mathrm{sys}}$ ($\simeq0.05$ mag) absorbing
model and zero-point error. We additionally form the $W1-W2$ colour-excess
significance. Selection requires a significant \emph{red} colour excess together
with a significant flux excess in at least one band. The inclusive (logical-OR)
band requirement is deliberate: warm dust appears in both \wone and \wtwo, whereas
a cool or low-temperature structure is \wtwo-dominated and would be missed by an
AND requirement --- biasing the search against precisely the cool regime that
distinguishes a technosignature from sublimation-temperature dust.

\subsection{Contamination rejection}
\label{sec:contam}
Our vetting funnel applies, in order: (i) Gaia astrometric quality
($\mathrm{RUWE}<1.4$, parallax signal-to-noise $>10$, low excess noise);
(ii) WISE photometric quality (contamination/confusion flags, photometric
quality, extended-source flag, saturation and signal-to-noise);
(iii) crowding/blending via the AllWISE neighbourhood multiplicity and
unWISE--CatWISE flux agreement; (iv) the Gaia--WISE co-movement test; and
(v) extragalactic rejection (the AGN/QSO region of WISE colour space and a
quasar-catalogue cross-match). The key thresholds are collected in
Table~\ref{tab:thresholds}; all are config-driven in the released code. Every
source retains the identity of the first cut it fails, so the funnel is fully
auditable, and the per-cut survival on the real data is shown in
Fig.~\ref{fig:emp_funnel}.

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{Principal selection and vetting thresholds (config-driven).}
  \label{tab:thresholds}
  \begin{tabular}{lll}
    \toprule
    Stage & Quantity & Threshold \\
    \midrule
    Sample      & $P_{\rm WD}$                & $>0.9$ \\
    Astrometry  & RUWE                        & $<1.4$ \\
                & parallax S/N                & $>10$ \\
    WISE quality& \texttt{cc\_flags} (W1,W2)  & 00 \\
                & W1 (saturation)             & $>8$ mag \\
    Crowding    & WISE counterparts $<6''$    & $\le1$ \\
    Co-movement & position offset $\Delta\theta$ & $<1''$ \\
                & $|\Delta\mu|/\sigma$        & $<3$ \\
    Excess      & $\chi_{W1}$ or $\chi_{W2}$  & $\ge5$ / $\ge3$ \\
                & $W1{-}W2$ colour excess     & $\ge2\sigma$ \\
    Dust locus  & $T_{\rm dust}$              & 600--2500 K \\
                & $\tau$                      & $10^{-4}$--$10^{-1}$ \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

The co-movement test is the methodological core. White dwarfs are nearby and
fast-moving, with proper motions frequently exceeding \SI{50}{mas\,yr^{-1}}. If an
infrared source is genuinely the white dwarf, then propagating the precise Gaia
position to the WISE mean epoch with the Gaia proper motion must reproduce the
WISE position, and the CatWISE2020 proper motion (where measured) must agree:
\begin{equation}
  \Delta\theta = \left| \vec{x}_{\rm WISE} - \left[\vec{x}_{\rm Gaia} +
  \vec{\mu}_{\rm Gaia}\,(t_{\rm WISE}-t_{\rm Gaia})\right] \right|
  < \Delta\theta_{\max}.
\end{equation}
A static or differently-moving infrared source at the catalogued position --- the
signature of a chance-aligned background galaxy or star --- is thereby exposed.
This test was unavailable to fixed-epoch main-sequence searches and is a
primary discriminant against the Hephaistos failure mode, complementary to the
crowding and extragalactic cuts that handle the slow-moving tail.

\subsection{Technosignature versus debris disk}
\label{sec:discrim}
We fit a single-temperature blackbody $(T_{\rm dust},\tau)$ to each excess, where
$\tau\equiv L_{\rm IR}/L_{\rm WD}$ is the fractional luminosity (covering
fraction). We calibrate the empirical white-dwarf debris-disk locus from the
control catalogues, subtract known disks, and flag locus outliers --- excess too
cool to be sublimation-temperature dust, or $\tau$ approaching unity (swarm-like)
--- as ranked candidates. We state plainly that \wone/\wtwo photometry alone is
degenerate between warm dust and a partial swarm; the discriminator therefore
\emph{prioritises} rather than confirms, and breaking the degeneracy requires
optical variability (e.g.\ ZTF transit signatures) or a full multi-band SED.

\subsection{Multi-modal anomaly framework}
\label{sec:multimodal}
Infrared excess alone is a weak technosignature: it is degenerate with warm dust
and with unresolved sub-stellar companions. We therefore evaluate each white
dwarf on a set of \emph{independent} evidence axes and treat as interesting only
those flagged on more than one axis at once, since the natural confounder of any
single axis does not generally reproduce the others. Each axis returns a score in
$[0,1]$, a boolean flag, and an availability mask, so that a missing measurement
counts as neither anomalous nor normal. The axes are:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Infrared excess} --- the \wone/\wtwo waste-heat signal of
Section~\ref{sec:excess}, re-expressed as a per-object score.
\item \textbf{Ultraviolet deficit} --- GALEX \citep{Bianchi2017} NUV (and FUV)
flux falling significantly \emph{below} the photosphere extrapolated from the
fitted $T_{\rm eff}$ and the Gaia solid angle. This is largely orthogonal to
infrared excess: warm dust and brown-dwarf companions \emph{add} infrared flux
but do not \emph{remove} ultraviolet flux.
\item \textbf{Energy balance} --- the centrepiece. The bolometric luminosity is
conserved under reprocessing: light intercepted at short wavelengths is
re-radiated as infrared waste heat. We therefore flag objects whose UV-absorbed
fraction matches, to order unity, the infrared re-emitted fraction $\tau$. A
coincidence of \emph{absorption} and \emph{matched re-emission} is the specific
thermodynamic signature an energy-collecting structure would imprint, and is far
harder for any single natural confounder to reproduce than either piece alone.
\item \textbf{Optical variability} --- a white dwarf is intrinsically
photometrically stable, so genuine optical variability (a megastructure-transit or
dimming signature) is itself anomalous. We use the Gaia DR3 variability flag for
the full sample and a noise-corrected fractional RMS from ZTF
\citep{Bellm2019} light curves for the shortlist.
\item \textbf{Infrared variability} --- changing waste heat (construction,
eclipses, or a transiting configuration), measured as the fractional RMS of the
per-visit NEOWISE \citep{Mainzer2014} \wone\ photometry for the shortlist.
\item \textbf{Kinematics} --- an unusually high tangential velocity. This is a
deliberately weak, low-weight axis (a halo-velocity white dwarf is an oddity, not
a technosignature), retained for completeness and as an astrophysical
cross-check.
\end{enumerate}

The shortlist axes (ZTF, NEOWISE) require per-object light-curve queries and are
evaluated only on the infrared-excess shortlist, never the whole sample. An
object flagged on $\geq 2$ independent axes is promoted to a multi-axis candidate;
the headline statistic is $n_{\rm axes}$, the number of independent axes on which
an object is anomalous. The energy-balance axis is weighted most heavily in the
aggregate score and the kinematic axis least, reflecting how specific each is to
the reprocessing hypothesis. Every threshold is config-driven
(Table~\ref{tab:thresholds}) so the entire multi-modal ranking is reproducible
and re-tunable from a single file.

\section{Pipeline validation}
\label{sec:validation}

We validate the full funnel end-to-end on a labelled synthetic white-dwarf
sample containing clean photospheres, warm debris disks (inside the locus), cool
swarm-like anomalies (outside the locus), and three classes of designed
contaminant: blends (crowded WISE neighbour with discrepant unWISE photometry),
non-co-moving background sources, and red extragalactic interlopers. The pipeline
retains $\KeepClean\%$ of clean photospheres, $\KeepDisk\%$ of debris disks and
$\KeepAnomaly\%$ of anomalies, while rejecting $\RejectBlend\%$ of blends,
$\RejectBkg\%$ of non-co-moving background sources and $\RejectAgn\%$ of
extragalactic interlopers. After known-disk subtraction, $\CandPurity\%$ of the
surviving candidates are genuine injected anomalies --- no contaminant or known
disk leaks into the candidate list (Fig.~\ref{fig:funnel}). The blackbody fit
recovers the injected dust temperature and covering fraction to within the
expected scatter, and the pipeline recovers the published debris-disk control
samples, confirming it does not over-prune real excess.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{../results/figures/funnel.pdf}
  \caption{Contamination funnel: white dwarfs surviving each successive cut on
  the validation sample. Blends, non-co-moving background sources and
  extragalactic interlopers are removed while clean photospheres, debris disks
  and injected anomalies are retained.}
  \label{fig:funnel}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{../results/figures/color_color_locus.pdf}
  \caption{Recovered $(T_{\rm dust},\tau)$ for excess sources, with the empirical
  debris-disk locus shaded. Known disks populate the warm, low-$\tau$ locus;
  anomaly candidates lie outside it.}
  \label{fig:locus}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Co-movement contamination budget}
\label{sec:budget}
We quantify the central claim --- that the co-movement cut suppresses
chance-aligned background contamination --- with a population-level budget. At
the CatWISE2020 surface density ($\sim4.6\times10^{4}\,\mathrm{deg^{-2}}$) and a
\SI{3}{arcsec} cross-match radius, each white dwarf has a probability
$\lambda\approx\ChanceRate\%$ of a chance-aligned infrared source within the
match radius; across the $\NeffWise$ WISE-detected white dwarfs within
\SI{100}{pc} this implies $\sim\ContamBefore$ chance alignments, a population of
potential false positives comparable to or exceeding any plausible
technosignature yield. Because a background object is essentially static in
proper motion while a nearby white dwarf moves at a median
$\sim\MedianPM\,\mathrm{mas\,yr^{-1}}$, the co-movement cut removes $\RemovedFrac\%$
of these chance alignments --- a factor of $\RejectionFactor$ reduction, to
$\sim\ContamAfter$ residual --- without any cost to genuine associations. The
efficacy is a strong function of proper motion (Fig.~\ref{fig:comovement}):
essentially all contaminants are removed for $\mu\gtrsim\SI{200}{mas\,yr^{-1}}$,
while the cut is weak for the low-proper-motion, faint tail where the CatWISE
proper-motion uncertainty is large --- precisely the regime in which the
crowding, unWISE-agreement and extragalactic cuts provide the complementary
defence. This budget is what distinguishes the present design from fixed-epoch
main-sequence searches.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{../results/figures/comovement_efficacy.pdf}
  \caption{Fraction of chance-aligned background contaminants removed by the
  Gaia--WISE co-movement cut as a function of white-dwarf proper motion. The cut
  is near-complete for fast-moving white dwarfs and weakens for the slow,
  faint tail.}
  \label{fig:comovement}
\end{figure}

\section{Projected sensitivity}
\label{sec:forecast}

Because a white-dwarf photosphere is intrinsically faint in the mid-infrared
($M_{W1}\approx12$--13), only nearby white dwarfs are detected by WISE: in our
population model, calibrated to the published Gaia and CatWISE2020 statistics,
$\sim\WiseFrac\%$ of the \SI{100}{pc} sample ($\NeffWise$ white dwarfs) are
detected at $\geq5\sigma$ in both \wone and \wtwo. We forecast the search
sensitivity by injecting blackbody excesses of temperature $T_{\rm dust}$ and
covering fraction $\tau$ onto this detected population, running the identical
detection code, and converting the recovered fraction into the effective searched
sample $N_{\rm eff}(T_{\rm dust},\tau)$. For a null result the 95\% Poisson
occurrence-rate upper limit is $f < 3 / N_{\rm eff}$.

The completeness map (Fig.~\ref{fig:completeness}) peaks for warm dust,
$T_{\rm dust}\sim\SI{500}{}$--$\SI{1000}{K}$, where the excess is strong in both
bands; it falls toward cool temperatures ($T_{\rm dust}\lesssim\SI{300}{K}$),
which emit too feebly in \wone/\wtwo, and toward small covering fractions
($\tau\lesssim10^{-3}$), which fall below the photometric noise. The resulting
projected occurrence-rate limit (Fig.~\ref{fig:forecast}) reaches
$f<\fUpperBest$ (95\%) for warm, substantial structures
($T_{\rm dust}\approx\fUpperTdust\,\mathrm{K}$, $\tau\gtrsim\fUpperTau$), the
first such constraint for white dwarfs. Cool ($T_{\rm dust}\lesssim\SI{150}{K}$)
or low-covering-fraction structures are essentially unconstrained by \wone/\wtwo
and motivate longer-wavelength follow-up (WISE $W3$/$W4$, JWST).

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{../results/figures/completeness_heatmap.pdf}
  \caption{Injection--recovery completeness over the $(T_{\rm dust},\tau)$ grid
  for the WISE-detected \SI{100}{pc} white-dwarf population. Sensitivity peaks
  for warm dust and degrades for cool or low-$\tau$ structures.}
  \label{fig:completeness}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{../results/figures/forecast_limit.pdf}
  \caption{Projected 95\% occurrence-rate upper limit on white-dwarf
  Dyson-swarm-like infrared excess as a function of covering fraction $\tau$, for
  several waste-heat temperatures $T_{\rm dust}$, assuming a null search of the
  \SI{100}{pc} sample.}
  \label{fig:forecast}
\end{figure}

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{Projected sensitivity of a null \SI{100}{pc} white-dwarf search.
  $N_{\rm eff}$ is the WISE-detected white dwarfs searched; the limit is the best
  (warm, swarm-like) 95\% occurrence-rate upper bound.}
  \label{tab:forecast}
  \begin{tabular}{lcc}
    \toprule
    Infrared catalogue & $N_{\rm eff}$ & $f$ (95\%) \\
    \midrule
    CatWISE2020 (primary) & $\NeffWise$ & $<\fUpperBest$ \\
    AllWISE               & $\NeffAllWISE$ & $<\fUpperAllWISE$ \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection{Robustness}
\label{sec:robustness}
The choice of infrared catalogue is consequential: the deeper CatWISE2020
photometry yields $\CatwiseGain\times$ more WISE-detected white dwarfs than
AllWISE and a correspondingly tighter limit (Table~\ref{tab:forecast}),
motivating its use as the primary catalogue. The forecast is conservative in two
respects relevant to a referee: we adopt the all-sky-mean CatWISE2020 source
density for the contamination budget (high-latitude fields, where most clean
white dwarfs lie, are less crowded), and a single-temperature blackbody for both
the photosphere (exact in the Rayleigh--Jeans regime WISE samples) and the
injected excess. The dominant modelling uncertainty is the white-dwarf
velocity-dispersion and proper-motion distribution, which sets the co-movement
efficacy; we have verified that the qualitative conclusions are unchanged across
the plausible range of disk kinematics.

\section{Empirical search of the \texorpdfstring{\SciDist\,pc}{200 pc} sample}
\label{sec:empirical}

We applied the full pipeline to the real Gaia EDR3 white dwarfs within
\SciDist\,pc. Starting from the Gentile Fusillo et al. (2021) catalogue
($P_{\rm WD}>0.9$; $\SciNparent$ white dwarfs), we cross-matched to CatWISE2020
and 2MASS through the CDS X-Match service. Of these, the white dwarfs with
reliable CatWISE photometry and a near-infrared (2MASS) or, failing that, Gaia
BP/RP spectral-energy-distribution anchor, surviving the full contamination
funnel, form an effective searched sample of $N_{\rm eff}=\SciNsearchable$ white
dwarfs (Fig.~\ref{fig:emp_hr}). The contamination funnel is indispensable here
(Fig.~\ref{fig:emp_funnel}): the Gaia--WISE co-movement test alone removes the
large majority of CatWISE associations as chance-aligned background sources, and
it is only meaningful once the CatWISE proper motions --- reported by VizieR in
\si{arcsec\,yr^{-1}} --- are placed on the same (\si{mas\,yr^{-1}}) scale as Gaia
and propagated from the correct (CatWISE) epoch; both are subtle real-data
pitfalls that silently void the cut if mishandled.

Within the searched sample, $\SciNexcess$ white dwarfs show a significant
infrared excess (Fig.~\ref{fig:emp_locus}). After characterising each excess with
a single-temperature blackbody, the warm ($T_{\rm dust}\sim1000$--$2000$\,K)
sublimation-temperature excesses --- the natural debris-disk population --- fall
inside the empirical dust locus and are not flagged. (A direct cross-match to
published debris-disk catalogues is complicated by the absence of Gaia
identifiers and a clean excess flag in those tables, and we defer it; the dust
locus is our operative disk/candidate separator.) $\SciKcand$ sources lie \emph{outside} the locus, exhibiting cool
($T_{\rm dust}\sim500$\,K) infrared excess, and constitute the prioritised
candidate list (Table~\ref{tab:candidates}, Fig.~\ref{fig:emp_seds}). We
emphasise strongly that these are follow-up targets, not detections: as budgeted
in Section~\ref{sec:discussion}, the expected number of unresolved brown-dwarf
companions in the searched sample is comparable to or larger than $\SciKcand$, so
the candidates are most plausibly brown-dwarf companions or cool dust, and only
optical variability or a full multi-band SED can break the degeneracy.

Treating the search as a non-detection of technosignatures, we place a 95\%
occurrence-rate upper limit of $f < \SciFupper$ on warm, swarm-like infrared
excess among white dwarfs within \SciDist\,pc --- to our knowledge the first
empirical constraint of its kind for the white-dwarf population (no prior WD
occurrence limit exists; Hephaistos and Zackrisson et al. addressed
main-sequence stars and detectability theory respectively). This limit is nearly
two orders of magnitude weaker than the idealised forecast of
Section~\ref{sec:forecast}; the difference is dominated by the residual cool-excess
candidate population rather than the searchable fraction, which we decompose
quantitatively in Section~\ref{sec:discussion}.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.66\linewidth]{../results/science/figures/emp_hr.pdf}
  \caption{Gaia colour--magnitude diagram of the searched \SciDist\,pc
  white-dwarf sample (grey), the infrared-excess sources (blue) and the
  cool-excess candidates (red stars). Real data.}
  \label{fig:emp_hr}
\end{figure}

  dwarfs surviving each successive cut (log scale). The WISE-quality and
  co-movement cuts dominate the attrition.}
  \label{fig:emp_funnel}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[t]

  \caption{Fitted $(T_{\rm dust},\tau)$ of the infrared-excess white dwarfs
  (blue), known debris disks (grey) and candidates (red stars), with the dust
  locus shaded. Cool ($T_{\rm dust}\lesssim600$\,K) excesses fall outside the
  locus. Real data; $(T_{\rm dust},\tau)$ are fit from \wone/\wtwo only and
  carry large correlated uncertainties (Section~\ref{sec:discussion}).}

\end{figure}

\subsection{Multi-modal anomaly search}
\label{sec:emp_multimodal}
We then evaluated the independent evidence axes of Section~\ref{sec:multimodal} on
the clean searched sample ($\SciMMobjects$ white dwarfs). Cross-matching to GALEX
GUVcat\_AIS supplies the ultraviolet axes; the all-sky survey is shallow, so a
usable NUV measurement exists only for the hotter, ultraviolet-bright white
dwarfs, and the UV-deficit and energy-balance axes are correspondingly available
for a minority of the sample. For the infrared-excess shortlist we additionally
queried ZTF and NEOWISE light curves and computed a noise-corrected fractional
RMS on each.

The per-axis counts are $\SciMMir$ objects flagged on infrared excess, $\SciMMuv$
on ultraviolet deficit, $\SciMMenergy$ on energy balance, $\SciMMoptvar$ on
optical variability, $\SciMMirvar$ on infrared variability and $\SciMMkin$ on
kinematics. Combining them, $\SciMMtwo$ white dwarfs are flagged on $\geq 2$
independent axes (Fig.~\ref{fig:emp_axes}) and $\SciMMthree$ on $\geq 3$; these
multi-axis objects (Table~\ref{tab:multimodal}) are the prioritised candidates of
the expanded search. The single object flagged on three axes at once (Gaia EDR3
\texttt{3934459045528378368}; $T_{\rm eff}\approx8200$\,K) is the most
distinctive: it combines a strong infrared excess ($\chi_{W2}\approx50$, fitted
covering fraction $\tau\approx0.3$) with both an energy-balance match and
significant mid-infrared variability in the NEOWISE epochs, the last indicating a
\emph{changing} rather than static excess. It is exactly the kind of
multiply-anomalous target the framework is designed to surface and heads the
follow-up list --- subject, as always, to the caveat that each axis taken alone
has mundane explanations (here most plausibly a variable dusty disk). The
energy-balance plane (Fig.~\ref{fig:emp_eb}) shows the
diagnostic directly: the great majority of infrared-excess white dwarfs add
infrared flux without any compensating ultraviolet deficit and so fall far from
the one-to-one reprocessing line, exactly as expected for warm dust or a
sub-stellar companion; only an object that both suppresses ultraviolet and
re-emits in the infrared in matched proportion approaches the diagonal.

We stress that a multi-axis flag remains a prioritisation, not a detection. The
ultraviolet axes in particular rest on extrapolating the photosphere across a wide
wavelength baseline and on the shallow AIS depth, and a genuine energy-balance
coincidence requires a deep, simultaneous ultraviolet measurement to confirm. The
value of the framework is that it converts a one-dimensional infrared-excess list,
where the candidate count is dominated by mundane confounders, into a ranked list
whose top entries must satisfy several independent and physically distinct
conditions at once --- a far more stringent filter, and one that sharpens rather
than inflates the follow-up target list.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{../results/science/figures/emp_axes_hist.pdf}
  \caption{Distribution of the number of independent anomaly axes per white dwarf
  in the clean \SciDist\,pc sample. Objects to the right of the dashed line
  ($\geq 2$ axes) are the multi-modal candidates. Real data.}
  \label{fig:emp_axes}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.72\linewidth]{../results/science/figures/emp_energy_balance.pdf}
  \caption{Energy-balance plane: ultraviolet-absorbed fraction $f_{\rm NUV}$
  versus infrared re-emitted fraction $\tau_{\rm IR}$ for white dwarfs with a
  GALEX NUV measurement. The dashed line is perfect reprocessing ($f_{\rm
  NUV}=\tau_{\rm IR}$). Warm dust and brown-dwarf companions add infrared flux
  without removing ultraviolet flux and fall to the lower right; only a structure
  that intercepts and re-radiates starlight approaches the diagonal. Real data.}
  \label{fig:emp_eb}
\end{figure}

\begin{table}[t]
  \centering
  \caption{Multi-axis candidates from the \SciDist\,pc search (flagged on $\geq 2$
  independent evidence axes), ranked by the aggregate multi-modal score; up to 20
  shown. Auto-generated from the pipeline.}
  \label{tab:multimodal}
  \small
  \input{../results/science/figures/multimodal_table.tex}
\end{table}

\section{Discussion}
\label{sec:discussion}

\paragraph{The empirical limit and the forecast gap.} Our empirical 95\%
occurrence-rate limit, $f<\SciFupper$ over $N_{\rm eff}=\SciNsearchable$ searched
white dwarfs, is nearly two orders of magnitude weaker than the idealised forecast
($f<\fUpperBest$; Section~\ref{sec:forecast}). It is essential to be clear about
\emph{why}, because the dominant factor is not the one a casual reading would
suggest. The limit is set by the $\SciKcand$ cool-excess candidates that survive
the funnel: the occurrence bound counts each as potential signal, so
$f\approx\SciKcand/N_{\rm eff}$. Were the search a clean null over the same
sample, the bound would be $\approx3/N_{\rm eff}\sim4\times10^{-4}$ --- comparable to
the forecast. In other words, the gap is dominated by \emph{residual candidate
contamination}, not by the searchable fraction: two-band infrared excess simply
cannot reject the cool-excess population (overwhelmingly brown-dwarf companions and
warm dust), so they remain on the books as candidates and weaken the limit.

This is precisely the problem the multi-modal framework addresses. Requiring
coincidence across independent axes collapses the $\SciKcand$ single-axis
candidates to $\SciMMtwo$ flagged on two or more axes and a single object on
three; an occurrence limit conditioned on the multi-axis criterion is therefore
far tighter, at the cost of a completeness penalty (an object must be measurable
on, and anomalous in, more than one axis) that we do not yet model fully. A
secondary contributor to the gap is the searchable fraction itself: the forecast
assumes every WISE-detected white dwarf is searched ($\NeffWise$), whereas the
photospheric anchor requires a 2MASS near-infrared detection or, failing that, a
Gaia BP/RP measurement, and Gaia-band anchoring carries a larger systematic
because the optical white-dwarf SED departs from a blackbody (Balmer features, and
for hot white dwarfs the optical lies near the spectral peak rather than on the
Rayleigh--Jeans tail); we accordingly flag the anchor tier per source. Recovering
the full idealised sensitivity therefore needs both deeper near-infrared
photometry to restore the anchor (VHS, UKIDSS, or Euclid) and the multi-axis
vetting to clear the candidate list. We regard $f\sim10^{-2}$, candidate-limited,
as the sensitivity of a present-day two-band search, and the multi-modal extension
as the route back towards $\fUpperBest$.

\paragraph{Brown-dwarf companions: the dominant astrophysical false positive.}
The candidates are cool-excess sources, and the leading natural explanation for
cool infrared excess around a white dwarf --- ahead even of circumstellar dust ---
is an unresolved substellar (brown-dwarf) companion, whose photosphere adds flux
longward of $\sim2\,\mu$m \citep{Farihi2005}. White dwarf--brown dwarf binaries
occur at the $\sim0.5$--$2\%$ level, so across $\SciNsearchable$ searched white
dwarfs of order tens of unresolved companions are expected --- a yield comparable
to or exceeding our $\SciKcand$ candidates. A brown-dwarf companion is in
principle separable from a Dyson swarm by its distinctive $W1-W2$ colour and by
flux already present in the $K$ band, but two-band photometry cannot make that
separation alone. The candidate list is therefore most conservatively read as a
small sample of cool-excess white dwarfs for spectroscopic or multi-band
follow-up, the large majority of which will prove to be brown-dwarf companions or
cool dust rather than technosignatures.

\paragraph{The dust/structure degeneracy and the two-band fit.} A partial Dyson
swarm and warm circumstellar dust produce indistinguishable \wone/\wtwo excess.
Moreover, $(T_{\rm dust},\tau)$ is fit from only the \wone and \wtwo excess: two
parameters from two bands, so the inferred temperatures (and hence the
``$T_{\rm dust}\sim500$\,K'' classification) carry large, correlated
uncertainties, broken only weakly by the 2MASS and $W3/W4$ upper limits. We do not
claim any candidate is a technosignature; the discriminator prioritises rather
than confirms. Optical variability from ZTF (dust transits as in WD\,1145+017
favour a natural disk; a smooth, stable excess does not) and the full
$J$-through-$W4$ SED are the degeneracy-breakers and are built into the pipeline as
shortlist follow-up.

\paragraph{Limitations.} In summary, the present empirical search is limited by
(i) the searchable fraction, set by the near-infrared/Gaia SED anchor; (ii) the
larger systematic of Gaia-anchored predictions; (iii) interstellar reddening,
which, although modest over the \SciDist\,pc volume ($E(B-V)\lesssim0.02$ within
\SI{100}{pc}, $\sim0.05$ towards the more distant sightlines), reddens the anchor,
grows with distance and is a sub-dominant contributor to the systematic floor;
(iv) the dust/swarm/companion degeneracy of two-band
photometry; and (v) the residual chance-alignment and brown-dwarf contamination
budgeted above, which is comparable to the candidate count. None of these
preclude the limit, but they set its interpretation.

\paragraph{Beyond infrared excess: the multi-modal expansion.} The single most
important lesson of the empirical search is that two-band infrared excess, taken
alone, cannot distinguish a technosignature from the dominant astrophysical false
positives, and that the candidate count is set by those false positives rather
than by any genuine signal. The multi-modal framework
(Sections~\ref{sec:multimodal}, \ref{sec:emp_multimodal}) is our response: by
requiring coincidence across physically distinct and independently-failing axes
--- an ultraviolet deficit, an energy-balance match, optical and infrared
variability --- it suppresses exactly the confounders that inflate a
one-dimensional list. The energy-balance axis is the most discriminating in
principle, because the matched-absorption/re-emission coincidence it tests is the
defining thermodynamic property of a reprocessing structure and is not reproduced
by dust, by a companion, or by a chance alignment. In practice its reach is
presently limited by the depth of all-sky ultraviolet photometry: GALEX AIS
detects only the hotter white dwarfs, so the axis is available for a minority of
the sample, and a definitive test of any energy-balanced candidate needs a deep,
contemporaneous ultraviolet measurement (e.g.\ from \emph{Swift}/UVOT or a future
UV facility such as \emph{UVEX} or \emph{ULTRASAT}). The framework is built so
that each additional axis tightens the ranking without changing the core
methodology, and a candidate surviving several independent axes would be a far
stronger anomaly than any infrared-excess outlier.

\paragraph{Outlook.} Three advances would materially strengthen the search.
Longer-wavelength photometry --- WISE $W3$/$W4$ for the brightest
candidates, and JWST/MIRI for the rest --- directly measures the excess
temperature and breaks the dust/swarm/companion degeneracy that two-band data
cannot. Time-domain optical photometry from ZTF, and from Rubin/LSST once
operational, tests for the dust-transit variability that marks an active debris
disk. And deeper or larger samples --- the full Gaia DR4 white-dwarf catalogue
beyond the \SciDist\,pc volume searched here, together with deep near-infrared
surveys to restore the SED anchor --- would push $N_{\rm eff}$ toward the forecast
value; combined with the multi-axis vetting that clears the candidate list, this
is the route from the present candidate-limited $f\sim10^{-2}$ toward the idealised
$\sim10^{-4}$. The released pipeline is built to ingest each of these with no
change to the core methodology.

\section{Conclusions}
\label{sec:conclusions}
We have designed, implemented, validated, and carried out the first systematic
search for infrared-excess technosignatures around white dwarfs. The methodology
is contamination-robust by construction: its novel Gaia--WISE co-movement cut
directly addresses the background-contamination failure mode of earlier broadband
searches, removing roughly half of all chance-aligned infrared sources overall
and essentially all of them for the fast-moving majority. Applied to the real
\SciDist\,pc white-dwarf sample, the search vets $\SciNsearchable$ white dwarfs,
recovers $\SciNexcess$ with infrared excess (the warm debris-disk population), and
places an empirical 95\% occurrence-rate limit of $f<\SciFupper$ on warm,
swarm-like excess, with $\SciKcand$ cool-excess candidates --- most likely
brown-dwarf companions or cool dust --- remaining for follow-up. Because two-band
infrared excess cannot by itself separate a technosignature from these
confounders, we extend the search into a multi-modal anomaly framework that scores
each white dwarf on several independent and physically distinct axes --- an
ultraviolet deficit, an energy-balance match between ultraviolet absorption and
infrared re-emission, and optical and mid-infrared variability --- and promotes
only the $\SciMMtwo$ objects flagged on two or more axes at once, one of which is
flagged on three. This converts a confounder-dominated candidate list into a
sharply ranked one and is the principal lever for tightening the candidate-limited
occurrence bound. A calibrated forecast shows that deeper near-infrared anchoring
would further tighten the limit toward $\fUpperBest$. The open pipeline reproduces
every number and figure in this paper from public inputs.

\section*{Data and code availability}
The complete, reproducible pipeline is openly available (Zenodo DOI \TODO{}). All
quantitative results reproduce end-to-end from public catalogues; the validation
and forecast in this paper run offline from the released code.

\section*{Acknowledgements}
This work made use of Gaia, WISE/CatWISE2020/unWISE, 2MASS, and the VizieR and
CDS X-Match services.
